questions.question: A hub has four hosts connected to it. Host A sends a unicast frame to Host B. Which hosts receive the frame?
options: Only Host B, because the hub reads the destination MAC address
Only Host A and Host B, because they are communicating
Host B, C, and D — every port except the ingress port
Only the host whose IP address matches the destination
questions.explanation: A hub is a physical-layer repeater with no MAC awareness. It regenerates the signal out of every port except the ingress port, so all attached stations — including Host C and Host D — receive every unicast frame, even frames not addressed to them.

questions.question: In a shared half-duplex Ethernet segment using CSMA/CD, what happens when two stations transmit at the same time?
options: The frame with the higher priority wins and the other is dropped
Both frames are destroyed by a collision; each station backs off and retransmits after a random delay
The switch buffers one frame and forwards it after the other completes
Both frames are forwarded; the receiver reconstructs the correct data from duplicates
questions.explanation: CSMA/CD detects simultaneous transmissions as a collision. Both frames are corrupted and discarded. Each transmitting station detects the collision, stops transmitting, sends a jam signal, waits a random backoff interval (exponential backoff algorithm), and then retransmits.

questions.question: A switch's MAC table contains the entry AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA → port 2. A frame arrives on port 4 with source AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA and destination BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB (not in the table). What two actions does the switch take?
options: Forward the frame only to port 2; do not update the table
Update the table: AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA → port 4; flood the frame to all ports except port 4
Filter the frame because the source matches an existing table entry
Drop the frame because the source port conflicts with the table
questions.explanation: The switch always learns from the source address first — updating the table to reflect that AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA is now reachable on port 4 (this may be logged as a MAC flap if the change is rapid). Because BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB is unknown, the switch floods the frame out all ports except the ingress port (port 4).

questions.question: Why do Ethernet broadcasts still reach every port on a switch even though the switch has learned all unicast MAC addresses?
options: Switches treat broadcast as a special EtherType and must forward it to the CPU
The destination ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff is never learned in the MAC table; the switch always floods it to all ports except ingress
Broadcasts are converted to unicasts after the first ARP exchange
Only Layer 3 routing can suppress broadcasts; switches must pass them unchanged
questions.explanation: The broadcast MAC address ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff is never stored in a switch's forwarding table as a destination. Per the forwarding rules, any frame with a broadcast destination is always flooded to every port except the ingress port. This is why replacing hubs with switches reduces unicast leakage but does not contain broadcast storms — VLANs, routers, or storm-control policies are required for that.

questions.question: A network administrator notices in switch logs that the MAC address CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC is alternating between port 5 and port 9 every few hundred milliseconds. What is this condition called and what are two likely causes?
options: Port mirroring; caused by SPAN sessions capturing traffic on both ports
MAC flapping; caused by an Ethernet loop in the Layer 2 topology or a misconfigured NIC bonding/teaming setup
ARP spoofing; caused by a man-in-the-middle attack intercepting frames
VLAN hopping; caused by 802.1Q double-tagging on an access port
questions.explanation: Rapid alternation of the same source MAC across two ports is called MAC flapping. The switch rewrites its table entry each time the MAC appears on a different port. Common causes include a physical Ethernet loop (the same broadcast frame arrives back on a different port), a misconfigured NIC bond or team where both members carry traffic independently, a VM migration glitch, or a duplicate MAC address on two different devices.

questions.question: A link shows late collisions on the half-duplex side and FCS errors on the full-duplex side, but physical cabling tests pass. What is the most likely fault and what is the correct fix?
options: A bad SFP transceiver on one end; replace the optic module
A duplex mismatch — one side auto-negotiated full-duplex, the other is forced to half-duplex; fix by setting both sides to the same explicit speed and duplex
A broadcast storm caused by a spanning-tree topology change; re-enable STP on the port
MTU mismatch causing fragmentation; increase the MTU on the full-duplex side to match
questions.explanation: Late collisions (collisions detected after the first 64 bytes) on a half-duplex interface and FCS/CRC errors on the connected full-duplex interface are the classic signature of a duplex mismatch. The full-duplex side keeps transmitting while the half-duplex side detects collisions after the jam-size window, producing truncated frames with bad FCS values. The fix is to set both the NIC and the switch port to the same speed and duplex setting, either both to auto-negotiation or both to an identical forced value.
